study.journal: Frat JP, Thille AW, Mercat A, et al. High-Flow Oxygen through Nasal Cannula in Acute Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure. N Engl J Med. 2015;372(23):2185-2196.

population_context.population_description: Consecutive patients 18 years or older admitted to ICU with acute nonhypercapnic hypoxemic respiratory failure (Pao2/Fio2 ≤300 mm Hg despite oxygen therapy at ≥10 liters per minute, respiratory rate >25 breaths per minute, Paco2 ≤45 mm Hg) without underlying chronic respiratory failure.
population_context.risk_group: Patients with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure not requiring immediate intubation
population_context.exclusions: Paco2 >45 mm Hg, exacerbation of asthma or chronic respiratory failure, cardiogenic pulmonary edema, severe neutropenia, hemodynamic instability requiring vasopressors, Glasgow Coma Scale score ≤12, contraindications to noninvasive ventilation, urgent need for intubation, do-not-intubate order

intervention.name: High-flow oxygen therapy through nasal cannula
intervention.parameter_values: Initial gas flow rate of 50 liters per minute with Fio2 of 1.0, subsequently adjusted to maintain Spo2 ≥92%. Applied for at least 2 calendar days, could then be switched to standard oxygen therapy.
intervention.timing: Within 3 hours after validation of inclusion criteria, applied continuously in ICU

comparator.description: Two comparators: (1) Standard oxygen therapy delivered continuously through nonrebreather face mask at flow rate ≥10 liters per minute adjusted to maintain Spo2 ≥92%; (2) Noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation delivered through face mask connected to ICU ventilator with pressure support, adjusted to obtain expired tidal volume 7-10 ml/kg, minimum 8 hours per day for at least 2 calendar days, with high-flow oxygen between sessions.

outcome_effect.outcome_name: Proportion of patients requiring endotracheal intubation within 28 days after randomization

strengths: Multicenter randomized controlled trial with 310 patients across 23 ICUs in France and Belgium
Prospective design with pre-specified criteria for intubation to reduce delayed intubation risk
Independent safety monitoring board and masked outcome assessment at coordinating center
Stratified randomization by center and cardiac insufficiency history
Regular on-site monitoring of centers for protocol adherence and data accuracy
Focused on nonhypercapnic acute hypoxemic respiratory failure, a more homogeneous population than prior studies
limitations: Open-label design with individual study assignments not masked to patients or treating physicians
Secondary outcome of mortality was not powered as primary outcome
Relatively high intubation rates even in high-flow oxygen group (38%)
Study excluded patients with hypercapnia, cardiogenic pulmonary edema, and other conditions, limiting generalizability
Post-hoc analysis for mortality outcomes increases risk of type I error

applicability.generalizability_notes: Findings apply to hemodynamically stable patients with acute nonhypercapnic hypoxemic respiratory failure without chronic underlying lung disease or cardiogenic pulmonary edema. Results may not generalize to hypercapnic respiratory failure, cardiogenic pulmonary edema, COPD exacerbations, or hemodynamically unstable patients.

quoted_sentences.design: We performed a multicenter, open-label trial in which we randomly assigned patients without hypercapnia who had acute hypoxemic respiratory failure and a ratio of the partial pressure of arterial oxygen to the fraction of inspired oxygen of 300 mm Hg or less to high-flow oxygen therapy, standard oxygen therapy delivered through a face mask, or noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation.
quoted_sentences.intervention: In the high-flow–oxygen group, oxygen was passed through a heated humidifier (MR850, Fisher and Paykel Healthcare) and applied continuously through large-bore binasal prongs, with a gas flow rate of 50 liters per minute and an Fio2 of 1.0 at initiation (Optiflow, Fisher and Paykel Healthcare). The fraction of oxygen in the gas flowing in the system was subsequently adjusted to maintain an Spo2 of 92% or more.
quoted_sentences.results: The intubation rate (primary outcome) was 38% (40 of 106 patients) in the high-flow–oxygen group, 47% (44 of 94) in the standard group, and 50% (55 of 110) in the noninvasive-ventilation group (P = 0.18 for all comparisons). The hazard ratio for death at 90 days was 2.01 (95% confidence interval [CI], 1.01 to 3.99) with standard oxygen versus high-flow oxygen (P = 0.046) and 2.50 (95% CI, 1.31 to 4.78) with noninvasive ventilation versus high-flow oxygen (P = 0.006).
quoted_sentences.outcome: In patients with nonhypercapnic acute hypoxemic respiratory failure, treatment with high-flow oxygen, standard oxygen, or noninvasive ventilation did not result in significantly different intubation rates. There was a significant difference in favor of high-flow oxygen in 90-day mortality.
